# AI Peer Review — P7 Capstone Manuscript

**Paper:** Internationalization and Firm Performance across 49 Asian and Pacific Economies: Institutional Regimes, Digital Capabilities, and Managerial Characteristics as Contingency Factors  
**Target journal:** Journal of International Business Studies (JIBS)  
**Review date:** 2026-05-22  
**Generated by:** ai-scientist-llm-review skill (3-reviewer format, AI Scientist v2 adapted for IB journals)

---

## REVIEWER A — Skeptical

### Summary
The paper provides impressive WBES microdata coverage (91,982 firms, 49 economies) and confirms an inverted-U I–P relationship with a robust turning point. However, the fundamental endogeneity problem — firms self-select into exporting, so the OLS estimate conflates causal internationalization effects with selection — is acknowledged but not empirically remedied. JIBS standards require at least one attempt to address identification.

### Scores

| Criterion | Score (1–4) |
|---|---|
| Originality | 3 |
| Quality | 2 |
| Clarity | 3 |
| Significance | 3 |
| Soundness | 2 |
| Presentation | 3 |
| Contribution | 3 |

**Overall:** 4/10  
**Decision:** Major Revision

### Detailed Comments

**[CRITICAL] FSTS endogeneity is not addressed empirically.**  
The Melitz (2003) self-selection mechanism predicts that high-productivity firms are more likely to export, creating upward bias in the ascending-limb OLS estimate. The paper acknowledges this in §5.5 ("WBES is a cross-sectional design") but offers no empirical remedy. P3 Vietnam used instrumental variables; P5 China used panel variation. For the 49-economy pooled analysis, at minimum a Heckman two-stage selection model should be estimated: Stage 1 (selection into exporting: FSTS > 0 vs. FSTS = 0) using WBES sampling region and industry dummies as exclusion restrictions; Stage 2 (ln LP ~ FSTS + FSTS² + IMR + controls). If IV identification is genuinely infeasible at scale, this must be argued in §3.3, not buried in §5.5.

**[CRITICAL] N drops 65% from M2 (84,910) to M11 (29,840) with no missing-data analysis.**  
The sample in M11 — the full three-way moderation model — is 29,840 firms. This is likely a non-random subsample driven by DAI, TCI, and manager variable availability (concentrated in recent 2019+ WBES waves and larger economies). A test of attrition bias is required: do M11 completers differ from M2 completers on FSTS, ln(LP), ICRV distribution, and country composition? If completers are systematically from wealthier, more-digital economies (Group I–II), the M11 results may not generalize to the 49-economy claim in the title.

**[MAJOR] The ICRV coefficient sign is counterintuitive and potentially confounded.**  
M10: β(ICRV_group) = +0.763 (p < .001). The positive sign implies that weaker-institution economies have higher nominal labor productivity — which the authors attribute to lower price levels. But this confound may extend to the ICRV interaction terms (FSTS×ICRV and FSTS²×ICRV), which are the actual institutional tests. Using PPP-adjusted productivity (available for most WBES waves via World Bank price deflators) as an alternative DV would test whether the ICRV interaction pattern is robust to the price-level confound.

**[MAJOR] Adj R² remains very low in non-FE specifications.**  
M11 adj R² = .067; M10 adj R² = .049. These are low for firm-level OLS even in cross-sectional settings. Country-year FE in M5 achieves adj R² = .677, but FE absorbs the institutional and digital capability between-country variation that is theoretically central. The paper's strategy of using non-FE specifications for moderator tests (M7–M11) is justified conceptually, but the low R² leaves open the possibility that unobserved country-level heterogeneity is driving the interaction effects rather than institutional regimes. The authors should test whether the key interaction results (FSTS×DAI, DAI×ICRV) survive partial FE specifications (e.g., industry × year FE without country FE).

**[MODERATE] DAI Tier 1-2 measurement gap should be acknowledged in the Abstract.**  
The key DAI result (FSTS×DAI = -0.614, FSTS²×DAI = +0.766 in M8) is interpreted as "digital adoption compresses the ascending limb and attenuates the descending limb." But DAI is limited to website presence (binary) and e-payment share — Tier 1-2 capabilities. The H3 interpretation requires at least Tier 3 (digital coordination, cloud-based ERP) to fully support the "coordination cost compression" mechanism. This measurement limitation should be stated in the Abstract: "digital adoption, measured by Tier 1–2 capabilities (website and electronic payment) in WBES, …" rather than implying broader digital capability.

---

## REVIEWER B — Balanced

### Summary
The data coverage is exceptional and the Lind-Mehlum inverted-U confirmation across all 11 models is a strong empirical contribution. H6 (DAI×ICRV) is the most novel finding but is underdeveloped in the Discussion. JIBS will require deeper engagement with IB theory and at least one attempt to address FSTS endogeneity.

### Scores

| Criterion | Score (1–4) |
|---|---|
| Originality | 3 |
| Quality | 3 |
| Clarity | 4 |
| Significance | 3 |
| Soundness | 3 |
| Presentation | 4 |
| Contribution | 3 |

**Overall:** 6/10  
**Decision:** Major Revision

### Detailed Comments

**[MAJOR] H6 (DAI×ICRV = +0.060, p = .012) is underdeveloped in Discussion.**  
This is the most novel finding in the paper — regime-contingent digital capability effects. Yet §5.3 covers it in under 200 words. Given that DAI×ICRV is the capstone hypothesis connecting the dissertation's CDCM model to multi-economy data, this deserves substantially more development:
- What is the marginal effect of a one-SD increase in DAI on ln(LP) at ICRV = 1 (Advanced) vs. ICRV = 5 (SIDS/Frontier)?
- A marginal effects plot with 95% confidence bands at ICRV = 1, 3, 5 would be the most effective way to communicate this finding
- The positive sign of DAI×ICRV (+0.060) means that digital adoption raises performance more in weaker institutional environments — this should be emphasized as the core digital-institutional complementarity result

**[MAJOR] IB theory engagement is insufficient for JIBS standards.**  
The theoretical framework (§2) draws primarily on Uppsala, RBV, Institutional Theory, and Upper Echelons — appropriate foundational theories. However, JIBS reviewers will expect:
- Dunning's OLI paradigm (Dunning, 2000) for the motivation of why export intensity should matter
- Mathews' (2006) LLL (Linkage-Leverage-Learning) framework for emerging-economy MNEs, which is directly relevant to the ICRV Group III–VI sample
- Fuller engagement with the institutional voids literature (Khanna & Palepu, 2010; Webb et al., 2020) for the SIDS and Frontier contexts

**[MAJOR] Paternoster z-tests across ICRV groups for H5.**  
The paper claims that "the turning point shifts from approximately 28% (Group I) to 55% (Group V-VI)" based on the M10 decomposition. A formal Paternoster (1998) z-test comparing the quadratic coefficients across ICRV groups would provide statistical confirmation that the TP differences are significant, not just directionally consistent. The companion papers (P3, P5) use this test; consistency is expected.

**[MAJOR] Female manager endogeneity caveat needs to be more prominent.**  
The female top manager premium (β = +0.185, ≈20% productivity lift) is reported as a clean treatment effect, but female managers are endogenously selected into higher-performing firms (and into firms with less discrimination — typically more profitable). The Buchhave et al. (2026) evidence supports the "exceptional retained talent" interpretation, but this is correlational evidence cited in support of an OLS coefficient. The limitation should be stated in the Abstract or §3: "the female manager premium is estimated by OLS and reflects the correlation between female management and labour productivity conditional on observable firm characteristics; causal identification requires either randomization or instrumental variables."

**[MODERATE] Report the H5 TP decomposition numerically.**  
The text states TP ≈ 28% (Group I) and ≈ 55% (Group V-VI) derived from M10 coefficients. Report these as formal calculations: TP_ICRV-i = −(β₁ + β₃×i) / (2×(β₂ + β₄×i)) where β₁, β₂ are FSTS/FSTS² coefficients and β₃, β₄ are FSTS×ICRV and FSTS²×ICRV. Showing the formula and plugging in ICRV = 1 vs. 5 makes H5 reproducible.

**Positive points:**
- Lind-Mehlum test with all four Haans et al. (2016) conditions applied rigorously across 11 models is exemplary
- The TP consistency across M2 (36.4%), M3 (33.8%), M5 (40.0%), M11 (34.6%) is the strongest evidence for the inverted-U claim
- The Vietnam PMI/Hormuz context paragraph (§6) is both analytically grounded and policy-relevant
- The CDCM framework connecting digital adoption to institutional context is a genuine theoretical contribution at the dissertation level

---

## REVIEWER C — Supportive

### Summary
An exceptional dataset and rigorous quadratic testing protocol. The inverted-U at TP ≈ 36% across all 11 models is a landmark result for Asian I–P research. Minor additions to address the two key gaps (endogeneity acknowledgment and H6 expansion) would make this a strong candidate for JIBS acceptance.

### Scores

| Criterion | Score (1–4) |
|---|---|
| Originality | 4 |
| Quality | 3 |
| Clarity | 4 |
| Significance | 4 |
| Soundness | 3 |
| Presentation | 4 |
| Contribution | 4 |

**Overall:** 8/10  
**Decision:** Minor Revision

### Detailed Comments

**[MAJOR] Heckman selection model as robustness check.**  
Even a single Heckman robustness check — using regional dummies (e.g., coastal/non-coastal indicator where available) as exclusion restrictions — would address the most common JIBS reviewer objection to cross-sectional WBES analysis. The P3 Vietnam IV results (available in the dissertation) could be cited as evidence that the country-specific IV estimates are consistent with the pooled OLS sign and direction, providing indirect support for the OLS results' robustness.

**[MAJOR] Expand §5.3 (DAI×ICRV) with marginal effects plot.**  
The DAI×ICRV = +0.060 (p = .012) result needs a Figure showing the marginal effect of DAI on ln(LP) at ICRV = 1 (Advanced: Singapore, Korea), ICRV = 3 (Emerging: India, Vietnam), and ICRV = 5 (Frontier/SIDS: Papua New Guinea, Vanuatu) with 95% confidence intervals. This would be the most effective visualization of H6 and would make the institutional-digital complementarity story accessible to JIBS readers.

**[MODERATE] Add TFP-based DV robustness in M2/M5.**  
Appendix B describes TFP production function coefficients. Using TFP residuals as the DV in M2 and M5 (at minimum) would demonstrate that the inverted-U finding is not specific to the ln(LP) operationalization and generalizes to TFP — a more defensible productivity measure that controls for factor inputs.

**[MODERATE] Oman sensitivity check.**  
Oman's exclusion from M3+ is explained (pre-digital 2003 wave) but not tested. Including Oman with DAI imputed from the 2003 global mean would show whether the Group II institutional estimate is sensitive to this exclusion.

**[MINOR] The Abstract should lead with the cross-model TP consistency.**  
Currently the Abstract opens with the N and specification. Recommending: "Across all 11 model specifications — from the baseline quadratic (M2, N = 84,910) to the full three-way moderation model (M11, N = 29,840) — the inverted-U turning point falls consistently between 33.8% and 40.0% of foreign sales intensity, confirming a robust inverted-U I–P relationship in Asia-Pacific."

**[MINOR] CDCM should be cited consistently.**  
The Context-Contingent Digital-and-Capability Model (CDCM) is referenced in §2.4 and §5.3 but not consistently capitalized or cited throughout. As a contribution of the broader dissertation programme, it should appear in the Abstract and be consistently referenced with "(Đỗ & Phan, 2024)" throughout.

**Positive points:**
- N = 91,982 across 49 economies and 102 waves is unprecedented for WBES-based I–P research
- The Haans et al. (2016) four-condition inverted-U test is applied correctly and completely
- The finding that TCI raises performance level without reshaping the curve (H2 partial support) is theoretically interesting — consistent with the absorptive capacity argument that TCI's curve-shaping role requires institutional complementarity
- The female top manager premium (≈20%) corroborated by Buchhave et al. (2026) and IFC (2022) adds policy relevance

---

## META-REVIEW

### Consensus
All three reviewers agree on:
1. **FSTS endogeneity is the primary unaddressed empirical threat** — JIBS standards require at least a Heckman selection model as a robustness check
2. **N drop from M2 (84,910) to M11 (29,840) needs missing-data analysis** — completers may not be representative of the full 49-economy claim
3. **H6 (DAI×ICRV, p = .012) is underdeveloped** in the Discussion for the most novel finding
4. **Female manager premium needs a more prominent endogeneity caveat**

### Key Disagreements
- Reviewer A rates quality 2/4 and soundness 2/4 (endogeneity and N-drop are disqualifying); B rates 3/4; C rates 3/4
- Reviewer A recommends Major Revision; C recommends Minor Revision; B recommends Major Revision
- On the overall score: A = 4/10, B = 6/10, C = 8/10

### Priority Revision List

| Priority | Item |
|---|---|
| CRITICAL | Add Heckman two-stage selection model as endogeneity robustness check (Stage 1: FSTS > 0; Stage 2: ln LP ~ FSTS + FSTS² + IMR + controls) |
| CRITICAL | Report missing-data analysis: do M11 completers (N = 29,840) differ from M2 completers (N = 84,910) on FSTS, ln(LP), ICRV distribution? |
| MAJOR | Expand §5.3 with marginal effects of DAI on ln(LP) at ICRV = 1, 3, 5 (Figure); calculate and report conditional slopes |
| MAJOR | Add formal Paternoster (1998) z-tests comparing inverted-U TP across ICRV groups (Group I vs. V–VI) |
| MAJOR | State female manager endogeneity caveat in Abstract or §3.3 (not only §5.5) |
| MAJOR | Add OLI (Dunning, 2000), LLL (Mathews, 2006), and institutional voids (Khanna & Palepu, 2010) to §2.1 theoretical foundations |
| MODERATE | Run M2/M5 with TFP-based DV (from Appendix B production function) as robustness check |
| MODERATE | Include Oman sensitivity analysis (M3+, imputed DAI = 2003 global mean) |
| MODERATE | Report TP formula numerically for H5 ICRV decomposition (TP at ICRV = 1 vs. 5) |
| MODERATE | Test FSTS×DAI and DAI×ICRV with partial FE (industry × year, no country FE) to distinguish between institutional regime effects and time trends |
| MINOR | Acknowledge DAI Tier 1-2 measurement limitation in Abstract |
| MINOR | Lead Abstract with cross-model TP consistency (33.8%–40.0% across M2–M11) |
| MINOR | Cite CDCM consistently (Đỗ & Phan, 2024) throughout |

### Final Recommendation
**Major Revision for JIBS.**

The data coverage (N = 91,982, 49 economies, 102 waves) and the cross-model robustness of the inverted-U (TP ≈ 36% across all 11 specifications) are genuinely exceptional. The DAI×ICRV finding (p = .012) is the most novel theoretical contribution and deserves fuller development. Heckman endogeneity check and missing-data analysis are non-negotiable for JIBS standards. Once these revisions are addressed, the paper has a strong publication case.

---

## IB-Specific Checklist

| Item | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| ICRV regime defined and operationalized | ✓ | 6-group classification; integer 1–6 used as moderator |
| Endogeneity addressed empirically | ✗ | Acknowledged in §5.5 but no Heckman or IV remedy applied |
| β + SE + CI reported | ✓ | HC1 robust SEs; Table 3 coefficients with significance stars |
| Lind-Mehlum u-test applied | ✓ | All four Haans et al. (2016) conditions checked across all models |
| Mechanism cited (theoretical grounding) | ✓ | Uppsala, RBV, Institutional Theory, Upper Echelons throughout |
| PRISMA / systematic search | N/A | Primary empirical study |
| OSF pre-registration | N/A | Not required for primary study at JIBS |
| Paternoster z-tests across subgroups | ✗ | Referenced in companion papers but not applied to ICRV groups |
| Marginal effects for interaction | ✗ | DAI×ICRV result (p = .012) needs marginal effects figure |
| Missing-data analysis (N dropout) | ✗ | M2 (N=84,910) → M11 (N=29,840) drop not analyzed |
